steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The team’s claim has two parts: (1) a genetic mutation strongly affects dog size, and (2) that mutation existed long before humans began deliberately breeding dogs (i.e., before 12,000 years ago). A supporting finding must address the timing of the mutation — showing it predates human breeding.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — The genetic mutation exists in an ancestor of domestic dogs dating to 53,000 years ago.: 53,000 years ago is well before the 12,000-year threshold for human breeding, directly confirming the mutation predated selective breeding. ✓
  • Choice B — Wolves are related to domestic dogs but are much bigger than Pomeranians.: a size comparison between wolves and Pomeranians says nothing about when the mutation arose. ✗
  • Choice C — Some domestic dog breeds that do not have the genetic mutation are very large.: this describes the mutation’s absence in large breeds, but doesn’t address the timing claim. ✗
  • Choice D — Dogs vary in size more than any other type of land mammal does.: overall size variation across species doesn’t speak to when this specific mutation appeared. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
